items.question: Who uses machine learning in practice?
items.answer: Data scientists and ML engineers build models; product and operations teams define tasks and metrics; domain experts label data; platform teams deploy trained models for inference on live traffic.

items.question: What is machine learning?
items.answer: Machine learning is a branch of AI where a system improves performance on a task by learning patterns from data—fitting parameters from examples instead of encoding every input-output rule by hand.

items.question: When should you choose ML over hand-written rules?
items.answer: When the input-output mapping is too complex, noisy, or fast-changing to maintain as explicit rules, and you have enough representative data to fit and evaluate a model.

items.question: Where does learning happen in an ML pipeline?
items.answer: During training: a learning algorithm updates model parameters using training data and a loss or likelihood objective; inference on new inputs uses those frozen parameters without further learning.

items.question: Why is generalization the central goal?
items.answer: Production systems see inputs not in the training file; a model that only memorizes training rows fails on new users, images, or text, so held-out evaluation measures whether learned patterns transfer.

items.question: How does a minimal supervised ML loop work?
items.answer: Collect labeled examples, split train/validation/test, choose a model family, train by minimizing loss on train data, tune on validation, report test metrics, then deploy the trained model for inference on new inputs.

items.question: How does machine learning differ from hand-written rule systems?
items.answer: Rules explicitly encode logic (if subject contains "winner" then spam); ML induces logic in parameters from labeled examples, scaling to messy language and evolving spam tactics without editing thousands of conditions.

items.question: Machine learning vs deep learning—which is broader?
items.answer: Machine learning is the umbrella (learn from data via any algorithm—trees, linear models, kernels, neural nets). Deep learning is ML restricted to neural networks with many layers, often trained on large raw inputs.

items.question: What happens when training data labels are systematically wrong?
items.answer: Cause: mislabeled or biased labels in training data. Effect: the model learns the wrong mapping and reproduces those errors at inference—high train accuracy but harmful or nonsensical predictions in production.

items.question: What happens when you evaluate only on training data?
items.answer: Cause: no held-out test set and metrics computed on the same rows used to fit parameters. Effect: reported performance is optimistically biased because memorization is mistaken for generalization.
